questions.prompt: How does TCP differ from UDP?

answers.text: TCP provides an ordered reliable byte stream.

feedback.legit: TCP provides an ordered reliable byte stream. Marking it Legit was correct.
feedback.sus: TCP provides an ordered reliable byte stream. This was accurate as written.

answers.text: UDP datagrams can be lost, duplicated, or reordered.

feedback.sus: UDP datagrams can be lost, duplicated, or reordered. This reply deserved a Legit vote.

answers.text: UDP avoids connection setup and lets applications choose their own reliability strategy.

feedback.sus: UDP avoids connection setup and lets applications choose their own reliability strategy. This one should stay marked Legit.

answers.text: It encrypts every application connection automatically.

questions.prompt: What does idempotent mean for an HTTP method?

answers.text: An idempotent request must return byte-for-byte identical responses every time.

feedback.legit: Idempotency concerns the intended server effect; response content can change between requests. The reply changes an important technical fact.
feedback.sus: Idempotency concerns the intended server effect; response content can change between requests. That makes your Sus choice accurate.

answers.text: Repeating the same request has the same intended effect as sending it once.

feedback.legit: Repeating the same request has the same intended effect as sending it once. You classified that correctly as Legit.
feedback.sus: Repeating the same request has the same intended effect as sending it once. This one should stay marked Legit.

answers.text: The request has no side effects.

feedback.legit: An idempotent request can change state; repetition should not add further intended change. The original claim needed a Sus vote.
feedback.sus: An idempotent request can change state; repetition should not add further intended change. Your vote matches the correction.

feedback.sus: Independent streams reduce transport-level head-of-line blocking between streams. The reply belongs under Legit.

questions.prompt: What problem does path MTU discovery address?

answers.text: Path MTU discovery matters only for IPv4 and never for IPv6.

feedback.legit: IPv6 also relies on path MTU discovery because routers do not fragment packets in transit. The original reply should be marked Sus.
feedback.sus: IPv6 also relies on path MTU discovery because routers do not fragment packets in transit. Sus was the correct call here.

answers.text: The largest MTU on any link is automatically safe for the whole path.

feedback.sus: The path MTU is constrained by the smallest supported MTU along the route. The Sus label fits the misconception.

answers.text: Choosing the fastest DNS resolver.
